metadata.source_file: Plot-Rice_v1_A global plot-based rice benchmark dataset with spatiotemporal heterogeneity for scientific deep learning.md
metadata.title: Plot-Rice: A plot-based global spatiotemporally heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset for deep learning
metadata.abstract: Ji Ge[a][,][b][,][c] , Hong Zhang[a][,][b][,][c][,][*] , Wenjiang Huang[b][,][c][,][d][,][*] , Zihuan Guo[a][,][b][,][c] , Lu Xu[a][,][b][,][c] , Yazhe Xie[a][,][b][,][c] , Mingyang Song[a][,][b][,][c] , Yinhaibin Ding[a][,][b][,][c] , Chao Wang[a][,][b][,][c]
metadata.introduction:
Rice, as a staple food, sustains more than half of the global population (Yuan et al., 2021). Utilizing advanced technologies to monitor the spatial distribution of rice on a large scale and in a timely manner yields critical foundational information for tasks such as crop condition monitoring and yield prediction (Sun et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2023). This facilitates the development of sustainable food production systems and strengthens food security, which is pivotal to achieving sustainable development goal 2 (SDG 2). 

Owing to the rapid advancement of satellite constellations such as 

synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and multispectral sensors, the volume and availability of satellite images have continually increased. This expansion has demonstrated the substantial value of deep learning (DL) models, which depend on substantial training data for applications such as large-scale rice mapping and growth monitoring in agriculture (Cao et al., 2021; Koklu et al., 2021; Thorp and Drajat, 2021). Due to their minimal susceptibility to weather conditions, SAR images have become a vital data source for closely monitoring crops. Numerous methods have also been dedicated to combining time-series SAR and polarimetric SAR to extract high-precision rice area distributions (Bazzi et al., 2019; Ge et al., 2023a; Ge et al., 2023b). The European Space Agency’s (ESA) 

- Corresponding authors at: International Research Center of Big Data for Sustainable Development Goals, Beijing 100094, China. _E-mail addresses:_ zhanghong@radi.ac.cn (H. Zhang), huangwj@radi.ac.cn (W. Huang). 

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jag.2025.104569 

Received 30 November 2024; Received in revised form 18 April 2025; Accepted 26 April 2025 Available online 13 May 2025 

1569-8432/© 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). 

> _J. Ge et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation 140 (2025) 104569_ 

Copernicus program through the Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 missions, offers free access to high-resolution dual-polarized SAR datasets and multispectral datasets, respectively. Compared to Landsat (30-meter spatial resolution) and MODIS (minimum 250-meter resolution) (Guan et al., 2018), both Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 provide a superior resolution of 10 m (Mandal et al., 2020). Furthermore, coupled with their excellent temporal resolution and moderate swath width, they effectively cover most global regions, delivering robust data support for rice distribution monitoring and deep learning algorithm development. However, existing datasets used for DL in rice distribution extraction often suffer from limited heterogeneity, excessive customization, and inadequate resolution, constraining the development of rice mapping research and the evaluation of DL model performance. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop high-quality benchmark datasets for rice to address the requirements of rice distribution mapping research, particularly in the context of long time-series and large-scale applications. 

The limited heterogeneity of rice datasets constrains the transferability of DL models. From a global perspective, rice exhibits both spatial morphological heterogeneity and temporal variation heterogeneity. Influenced by topographical conditions, land planning, agricultural management practices, and other anthropogenic factors, the spatial morphology of rice differs across regions (Ge et al., 2025). In Asia, large plains or river valleys form extensive continuous rice fields. However, at a local scale, these fields are fragmented into irregular small plots. Spatially, rice fields display characteristics of local irregularity alongside an overall dense and extensive distribution (Sun et al., 2022; Xiao et al., 2021). In developed regions such as Europe and America, agricultural planting is highly mechanized (Nowak, 2021), thus rice fields there tend to be locally uniform and generally densely distributed. In less developed regions like Africa, agricultural planting ranges from small-scale family farms to large-scale commercial farms (Abubakar et al., 2021), leading to considerable internal spatial variability within rice fields, encompassing both large continuous areas and small, scattered plots, often of irregular shape. 

Additionally, climatic conditions, irrigation practices, and soil fertility are significant factors contributing to the temporal heterogeneity of rice cultivation systems (Mishra et al., 2021). As latitude decreases, the intensity of rice cultivation increases, varying from singleseason to triple-season rice. In regions like Southeast Asia, the coexistence of single-season, double-season, and triple-season rice is more common (Han et al., 2022), further exacerbating the temporal heterogeneity of rice. Consequently, although DL can achieve high precision in extracting rice distribution over small areas (Thorp and Drajat, 2021; Zuo et al., 2024), the lack of learning about the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of rice often results in a lack of transferability in DL models. 

The excessive customization in datasets creates usage barriers that reduce both the usability of the dataset and the generalization capability of the DL models. The rice samples used for DL training mainly come from two sources: manual annotation and existing products. Many studies have acquired highly accurate rice samples for DL training through field surveys and manual annotations (Ramadhani et al., 2020; Xia et al., 2022). However, due to the substantial costs of annotation, the scope of these studies is typically limited to farm-level or municipallevel areas (Bazzi et al., 2019; Zuo et al., 2024), resulting in incomplete coverage of all planting conditions. This limitation leads to reduced generalization ability of DL models, making them difficult to transfer to other regions. Moreover, the datasets and classification models in such studies are excessively customized, posing significant barriers to reuse. The existence of multiple customized datasets within the same typical planting area has led to unnecessary duplication of effort and resource wastage (Gao et al., 2023; Pang et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2024). 

Some studies utilized existing rice distribution mapping products as labels and constructed datasets by extracting diverse features from satellite images for training DL models (Hao et al., 2020; Li et al., 2024a; 

Wei et al., 2021). However, the limited resolution of these existing products is insufficient to meet the needs for fine-scale rice mapping, failing to fully leverage the resolution advantages of the Sentinel satellite series, thus substantially hindering the development of large-scale rice distribution mapping. Additionally, existing large-scale rice mapping products generally employ classification methods such as dynamic time warping (DTW) and machine learning (Gao et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2023; Song et al., 2024). These pixel-based classification methods neglect the spatial morphological characteristics of rice, resulting in indistinct boundaries of rice fields and causing a discretized distribution during the classification process. When such products are directly used for training DL models, they inevitably reduce the classification accuracy and the quality of prediction results. It is also important to consider the errors introduced during the registration and resampling processes of satellite data and product labels. Therefore, to address the common issues of limited heterogeneity, excessive customization, and low resolution in current rice satellite datasets, there is an urgent need for a benchmark dataset that provides a unified data foundation for evaluating DL models. This would fill the long-standing gap in benchmark datasets within the field and effectively reduce redundant efforts caused by data processing. 

To address challenges arising from the absence of high-quality rice benchmark datasets, including limited spatiotemporal heterogeneity, excessive customization, and inadequate resolution of rice distribution data, this research establishes a plot-based global spatiotemporally heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset, named Plot-Rice to support scientific deep learning, standardize algorithm evaluation benchmarks, and lower research barriers. The Plot-Rice dataset consists of 13,552 high-resolution rice sample patches at 10-meter resolution, covering 123 rice-growing regions with spatiotemporal heterogeneity across Asia, Europe, Africa, the Americas, and Oceania. Regarding feature composition, the dataset provides time-series data covering 12 months of 2023, with each timepoint comprising 10 multi-source features extracted from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2. These features include fundamental radar backscattering coefficients, radar vegetation indices, normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI), land surface water index (LSWI), spectral combinations, as well as more computationally complex features such as normalized radar backscattering coefficients and DpRVIc. With its excellent scalability, Plot-Rice allows users to further analyse and extract derivative features based on the fundamental features and their time series to meet diverse research requirements. 

To resolve the current inefficiency in rice sample labelling and address issues of poor internal consistency and indistinct boundaries when existing products are directly used as labels, this research designs an efficient rice sample generation framework. This framework leverages the segment anything model 2 (SAM-2) (Ravi et al., 2024), utilizing its advanced segmentation capabilities to assist in identifying field boundaries. To overcome the limitation that SAM-2 cannot directly classify, this research integrates SAR time-series analysis and machine learning methods to assign categories at the plot level. This approach significantly improves sample extraction efficiency while ensuring highquality labels with clear boundaries. The Plot-Rice dataset and its generation framework provide a standardized data foundation and reproducible sample generation process for deep learning-based rice mapping research, laying a robust foundation for large-scale rice monitoring research.

evidence.original_text: To address these deficiencies, this paper introduces Plot-Rice v1.0, a global heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 images, along with an automated framework that integrates SAR temporal features and leverages the foundation image segmentation model SAM-2 to generate plot-level rice samples. This framework effectively addresses the limitation of SAM-2, which supports zero-shot segmentation but lacks classification capabilities. Plot-Rice comprehensively accounts for the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of rice, incorporating plot-level rice labels and corresponding multi-source feature time series from 20 countries globally. In terms of feature composition, Plot-Rice integrates multi-source features, including radar backscattering coefficients, normalized backscattering coefficients, radar vegetation indices, spectral indices, and spectral combinations, constructing time series for 12 months of 2023 to facilitate temporal analysis. Finally, using this dataset, a series of benchmark experiments is conducted, encompassing performance comparisons of models such as U-Net, Res U-Net, Deeplabv3+, and HR-Net under multi-source feature time series, and comparisons of rice distribution extraction performance across diverse countries. Several recommendations are summarized for artificial intelligence (AI) in rice mapping to serve as a reference, contributing to global food security solutions.

Satellite platforms have become the principal data source for large-scale rice mapping. Numerous studies investigated the advantages of integrating deep learning models with satellite data for rice mapping, but these studies are typically confined to specific regions and the quality of customized datasets varies. The absence of a global, standardized satellite dataset for rice mapping benchmarking has long resulted in both substantial redundant data processing efforts and challenges in evaluating new algorithms under a unified benchmark. To address these deficiencies, this paper introduces Plot-Rice v1.0, a global heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 images, along with an automated framework that integrates SAR temporal features and leverages the foundation image segmentation model SAM-2 to generate plot-level rice samples. This framework effectively addresses the limitation of SAM-2, which supports zero-shot segmentation but lacks classification capabilities. Plot-Rice comprehensively accounts for the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of rice, incorporating plot-level rice labels and corresponding multi-source feature time series from 20 countries globally. In terms of feature composition, Plot-Rice integrates multi-source features, including radar backscattering coefficients, normalized backscattering coefficients, radar vegetation indices, spectral indices, and spectral combinations, constructing time series for 12 months of 2023 to facilitate temporal analysis. Finally, using this dataset, a series of benchmark experiments is conducted, encompassing performance comparisons of models such as U-Net, Res U-Net, Deeplabv3+, and HR-Net under multi-source feature time series, and comparisons of rice distribution extraction performance across diverse countries. Several recommendations are summarized for artificial intelligence (AI) in rice mapping to serve as a reference, contributing to global food security solutions.

In terms of feature composition, Plot-Rice integrates multi-source features, including radar backscattering coefficients, normalized backscattering coefficients, radar vegetation indices, spectral indices, and spectral combinations, constructing time series for 12 months of 2023 to facilitate temporal analysis. Finally, using this dataset, a series of benchmark experiments is conducted, encompassing performance comparisons of models such as U-Net, Res U-Net, Deeplabv3+, and HR-Net under multi-source feature time series, and comparisons of rice distribution extraction performance across diverse countries. Several recommendations are summarized for artificial intelligence (AI) in rice mapping to serve as a reference, contributing to global food security solutions.

To address these deficiencies, this paper introduces Plot-Rice v1.0, a global heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 images, along with an automated framework that integrates SAR temporal features and leverages the foundation image segmentation model SAM-2 to generate plot-level rice samples. Plot-Rice comprehensively accounts for the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of rice, incorporating plot-level rice labels and corresponding multi-source feature time series from 20 countries globally. In terms of feature composition, Plot-Rice integrates multi-source features, including radar backscattering coefficients, normalized backscattering coefficients, radar vegetation indices, spectral indices, and spectral combinations, constructing time series for 12 months of 2023 to facilitate temporal analysis.

The Plot-Rice dataset contains 13,552 sample patches of 256 × 256 pixel size, with each patch including 10 feature time series and 1 plot-level label. The time series corresponds to the 12 months of 2023 through monthly averaging, establishing a uniform temporal baseline and eliminating anomalous fluctuations in the time sequences. For each month, 10 features are extracted from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2, including σ0 VH, σ0 VV, γ0 VH, γ0 VV, DpRVIc, RVI, NDVI, LSWI, true-colour images and false-colour images. During the slicing process, the patch overlap rate is set to 0.2 to maintain the completeness of rice plot boundaries. In addition, completely background (no rice distribution) patches or low-quality patches are excluded. All patches are shuffled and split into 60 % for training, 20 % for validation, and 20 % for testing model performance, thereby constructing the final Plot-Rice dataset for scientific deep learning.

Auxiliary datasets including ESA WorldCover (Zanaga et al., 2022), CROPGRIDS (Tang et al., 2024) and GWL_FCS30 (Zhang et al., 2024) are also utilized. ESA WorldCover is a global land cover product based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data from the year 2021, offering 10-meter resolution. It includes 11 land cover categories and has been independently validated. This dataset is especially valuable for distinguishing between cultivated and non-cultivated land. CROPGRIDS is a comprehensive global georeferenced dataset that provides area information for 173 crops, including rice, for the year 2020. Although its spatial resolution is limited to 0.05◦ , it can still assist in identifying the approximate locations of rice cultivation. GWL_FCS30 provides global annual distribution maps of wetlands at a 30-meter resolution from 2000 to 2022, which are used to eliminate wetland pixels from pixel-level rice samples, thereby further ensuring the purity of the rice samples.

evidence.section: 3.1. Sentinel-1 SAR data and Sentinel-2 multispectral data
evidence.original_text: In this study, a total of 3055 Sentinel-1 SAR interferometric wide (IW) swath mode images is acquired throughout 2023. These images undergo the following processing steps as shown in Fig. 3(a): 1) Orbit file application: updating initial orbit data using precise orbit determination (POD); 2) Thermal noise removal: eliminating the effects of system thermal noise; 3) Radiometric calibration: converting pixel values to backscatter intensity (sigma nought) using sensor calibration parameters; 4) Multi-temporal registration: precisely aligning SAR images with DEM assistance to ensure spatial consistency across the time series; and 5) Geometric correction: applying the Range-Doppler terrain correction algorithm combined with DEM for precise geometric correction, resulting in a final resolution of 10 m. Furthermore, to meet the requirements for quantitative research, additional radiometric terrain corrections (RTC) (Flores-Anderson et al., 2023) are performed to provide normalized backscatter coefficients as features.

entries.description: Standardized preprocessing steps for Sentinel-1 SAR data including orbit correction, noise removal, calibration, registration, and geometric correction.

evidence.section: 3.1. Sentinel-1 SAR data and Sentinel-2 multispectral data
evidence.original_text: Furthermore, 3174 multispectral images from Sentinel-2 are acquired throughout the year 2023. These images are directly acquired from atmospherically corrected and geometrically corrected L2A level products. Beyond this, additional processing steps are performed as shown in Fig. 3(b): 1) Cloud and cloud shadow removal: identifying and excluding areas covered by clouds and cloud shadows based on quality assessment bands; 2) Multi-temporal composition: reducing cloud coverage effects through median compositing of multiple images within each month; and 3) Missing value filling: for any remaining data gaps, interpolation using nearby valid data is performed to ensure data accuracy and reliability for further analysis. The output resolution is also 10 m.

entries.description: Standardized preprocessing steps for Sentinel-2 multispectral data including cloud removal, temporal compositing, and missing value filling.

evidence.original_text: As shown in Table 2 and Fig. 5, to meet the diverse research needs, the following features are extracted from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data: σ0 VH, σ0 VV, γ0 VH, γ0 VV, DpRVIc (Bhogapurapu et al., 2022; Bhogapurapu et al., 2021), RVI (Trudel et al., 2012), the optical indices normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) (D’Odorico et al., 2013) and land surface water index (LSWI) (Wang et al., 2020b), as well as the truecolour images and the RiceTColour false-colour images (Li et al., 2024b).

entries.description: List of extracted features from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data for rice mapping.

evidence.original_text: Radar backscatter coefficient is the most fundamental feature in SAR data, and many studies have utilized multi-temporal backscatter coefficients (σ0) to extract rice areas (Bazzi et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021). Nevertheless, few studies have explored the use of normalized backscatter coefficients (γ0). The γ0, which applies angle-based correction methods to remove the influence of terrain on radar backscatter signals, can significantly improve the extraction of rice areas on sloped terrains (Mullissa et al., 2021). The RTC necessitates consideration of the relationship between terrain geometry and radar imaging geometry. An approximation can be obtained through the following expression: Where， θi represents the viewing incidence angle. In vegetation areas with terrain undulations, it is necessary to reference the volumescattering optimised physical model (Samuele et al., 2021) of the angular relationship between SAR imagery and terrain geometry to further modulate γ0: Where, γ0 f is the normalized backscattering coefficient for flat terrain. αr is the slope steepness angle in range direction, which is calculated based on terrain geometry modelling, with details provided in the Section S3 of the supplementary materials.

entries.description: Method for calculating normalized radar backscattering coefficient (γ0) using terrain geometry.

evidence.section: 4.3. Pixel-level rice label generation with temporal features and machine learning
evidence.original_text: Rice, as a semi-aquatic plant, is the only major crop that inundates its fields during the early growth stages, and its growth process exhibits unique phenological characteristics in radar backscatter time series curves. Through the phenological analysis of VH time-series backscatter — coefficients, four temporal features vegetation phase index (VI), dynamic phase index (DI), water inundation phase index (WI), and the time-series mean (σ0 mean)—are extracted to characterize the development of rice and facilitate pixel-level rice extraction. A rice probability threshold of 0.9 is used to identify high-confidence rice pixels, which are subsequently utilized as training samples for the random forest model. The high-purity attributes of these samples ensured the accuracy and reliability of the model in pixel-level classification. In addition, the wetlands, which also contain aquatic vegetation, exhibit similar behavioural characteristics to rice in the backscatter coefficient time series. Relying solely on the three aforementioned indices is insufficient to effectively distinguish between wetlands and rice. Through time-series curve analysis, it can be observed that wetlands remain in a water environment for a longer period than rice, leading to a lower temporal mean backscatter coefficient. Therefore, the GWL_FCS30 wetland distribution map is used as a mask to further eliminate potential wetland pixels from the rice samples, while the temporal mean (σ0 mean) is introduced as a feature to distinguish between wetlands and rice. Finally, by combining the four extracted temporal features and the confirmed samples, a random forest model is applied to extract rice, resulting in pixel-level rice labels.

entries.description: Methodology for generating pixel-level rice labels using temporal features and a random forest model.

evidence.section: 4.4. Plot-level label generation with segment anything model
evidence.original_text: Both the initially extracted rice labels and existing rice mapping products are classified at the pixel level, which results in the fragmentation of rice distribution and incomplete field boundaries. To address this issue, the segment anything model-2 (SAM-2) is introduced for plot-level rice label generation. The SAM-2 is a foundation model designed to address prompt-based visual segmentation problems in images and videos. Through training on 1 million images and 11 billion masks, it can perform zero-shot segmentation of unseen objects without being limited to specific categories or domains. However, the plot masks generated by SAM-2 lack assigned class information. To address this, the mask arrays are first converted into polygon vector shapes, and then the pixel-level results from the RF model are used to assign class to the plot boundary polygons based on rice coverage. If the rice coverage exceeds 50 %, the polygon is classified as rice; otherwise, it is classified as non-rice. To ensure the accuracy of the rice labels, manual validation and correction of the attributes of the polygons are conducted. During this process, the VH backscatter coefficient time series and the RiceTColour false-colour images are used to jointly verify the correctness of the rice labels. Any identified discrepancies prompted editing and correction of the polygon attributes as necessary. After manual verification and correction of the plot-level polygons, the polygons are converted to raster format to generate plotlevel rice labels, which are used for the construction of the final dataset.

entries.description: Methodology for generating plot-level rice labels using SAM-2 segmentation and pixel-level label integration.

evidence.section: 4.6. Evaluation of datasets based on deep learning models
evidence.original_text: The proposed dataset is evaluated using several state-of-the-art DL models, such as U-Net (Azad et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2022), Res U-Net (Diakogiannis et al., 2020; Onojeghuo et al., 2023), Deeplabv3+ (Chen et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2022), and HR-Net (Rasheed and Mahmood, 2023; Wang et al., 2020a). In this study, the dice loss is used as the loss function to overcome the imbalance in training classes (Xu et al., 2023). Dice loss assigns equal importance to false positives and false negatives and is less sensitive to class imbalances. All models in the experiments are implemented using Torch 1.11, CUDA 11.7, and cuDNN 8.2, and are trained and evaluated on a Linux workstation equipped with an NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU with 24 GB of VRAM. Adam is selected as the optimizer, with an initial learning rate of 0.001 and a learning rate decay strategy. Each model has a batch size of 16, and training is conducted for 200 epochs.

entries.description: Benchmark evaluation protocol using deep learning models (U-Net, Res U-Net, Deeplabv3+, HR-Net) with specific training parameters.

evidence.original_text: The overall sample generation framework is illustrated in Fig. 4. First, the Sentinel-1 VH/VV dual-polarized SAR data and Sentinel-2 multispectral data for the entire year of 2023 are collected based on the study areas. The acquired satellite data undergo pre-processing and feature extraction, concurrently constructing the corresponding feature time series. Subsequently, based on the phenological analysis, three temporal features vegetation phase index (VI), dynamic phase index (DI), and water inundation phase index (WI)—are derived. These features are combined to represent the probability of rice, with a stringent threshold (0.9) set to extract accurate rice pixel samples. Furthermore, the temporal mean feature (σ0mean) is incorporated to suppress interference from wetlands. By combining these temporal features with a random forest (RF) model, pixel-level rice labels are extracted. Next, to generate plot-level samples, SAM-2 is used to perform zero-shot segmentation on contrast-enhanced Sentinel-2 cloudless true-colour images, yielding plot masks that are subsequently converted into unlabelled polygon vectors. By incorporating pixel-level rice labels, the polygons in the vectors are assigned the rice class. Finally, all rice polygons are manually validated and corrected, converted into raster format, and processed into patches. The final Plot-Rice dataset consists of multiple feature time series and plot-level labels, characterized by multi-temporal, multi-feature, and spatiotemporal heterogeneity.

The SAM-2 is a foundation model designed to address prompt-based visual segmentation problems in images and videos. Through training on 1 million images and 11 billion masks, it can perform zero-shot segmentation of unseen objects without being limited to specific categories or domains. As shown in Fig. 7, the SAM-2 model structure consists of three components: an image encoder, a prompt encoder, and a mask decoder. During inference, the input image is processed through the image encoder to generate image embeddings, while point prompts are automatically generated and processed through the prompt encoder to obtain prompt embeddings. Both embeddings are then input into the mask decoder to produce segmentation masks. Specifically, the Hiera encoder consists of hierarchical vision transformer blocks is used to extract multi-scale features from images, serving as the image encoder of SAM-2 (Ryali et al., 2023). The prompt encoder performs positional encoding on the coordinates of the generated point prompts, ensuring compatibility with image features. The mask decoder uses a two-way transformer to fuse image features and prompt information to obtain coarse masks, which are then up-sampled through Up-scaled operations, and finally output through MLP layers as masks.

A rice probability threshold of 0.9 is used to identify high-confidence rice pixels, which are subsequently utilized as training samples for the random forest model. The high-purity attributes of these samples ensured the accuracy and reliability of the model in pixel-level classification. In addition, the wetlands, which also contain aquatic vegetation, exhibit similar behavioural characteristics to rice in the backscatter coefficient time series. Relying solely on the three aforementioned indices is insufficient to effectively distinguish between wetlands and rice. Through time-series curve analysis, it can be observed that wetlands remain in a water environment for a longer period than rice, leading to a lower temporal mean backscatter coefficient. Therefore, the GWL_FCS30 wetland distribution map is used as a mask to further eliminate potential wetland pixels from the rice samples, while the temporal mean (σ0mean) is introduced as a feature to distinguish between wetlands and rice. Finally, by combining the four extracted temporal features and the confirmed samples, a random forest model is applied to extract rice, resulting in pixel-level rice labels.

In this study, a total of 3055 Sentinel-1 SAR interferometric wide (IW) swath mode images is acquired throughout 2023. These images undergo the following processing steps as shown in Fig. 3(a): 1) Orbit file application: updating initial orbit data using precise orbit determination (POD); 2) Thermal noise removal: eliminating the effects of system thermal noise; 3) Radiometric calibration: converting pixel values to backscatter intensity (sigma nought) using sensor calibration parameters; 4) Multi-temporal registration: precisely aligning SAR images with DEM assistance to ensure spatial consistency across the time series; and 5) Geometric correction: applying the Range-Doppler terrain correction algorithm combined with DEM for precise geometric correction, resulting in a final resolution of 10 m. Furthermore, to meet the requirements for quantitative research, additional radiometric terrain corrections (RTC) (Flores-Anderson et al., 2023) are performed to provide normalized backscatter coefficients as features.

Furthermore, 3174 multispectral images from Sentinel-2 are acquired throughout the year 2023. These images are directly acquired from atmospherically corrected and geometrically corrected L2A level products. Beyond this, additional processing steps are performed as shown in Fig. 3(b): 1) Cloud and cloud shadow removal: identifying and excluding areas covered by clouds and cloud shadows based on quality assessment bands; 2) Multi-temporal composition: reducing cloud coverage effects through median compositing of multiple images within each month; and 3) Missing value filling: for any remaining data gaps, interpolation using nearby valid data is performed to ensure data accuracy and reliability for further analysis. The output resolution is also 10 m.

As shown in Table 2 and Fig. 5, to meet the diverse research needs, the following features are extracted from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data: σ0VH, σ0VV, γ0VH, γ0VV, DpRVIc (Bhogapurapu et al., 2022; Bhogapurapu et al., 2021), RVI (Trudel et al., 2012), the optical indices normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) (D’Odorico et al., 2013) and land surface water index (LSWI) (Wang et al., 2020b), as well as the true-colour images and the RiceTColour false-colour images (Li et al., 2024b). Radar backscatter coefficient is the most fundamental feature in SAR data, and many studies have utilized multi-temporal backscatter coefficients (σ0) to extract rice areas (Bazzi et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2021). Nevertheless, few studies have explored the use of normalized backscatter coefficients (γ0). The γ0, which applies angle-based correction methods to remove the influence of terrain on radar backscatter signals, can significantly improve the extraction of rice areas on sloped terrains (Mullissa et al., 2021). The RTC necessitates consideration of the relationship between terrain geometry and radar imaging geometry. An approximation can be obtained through the following expression: γ0 = σ0 / cosθi. Where, θi represents the viewing incidence angle. In vegetation areas with terrain undulations, it is necessary to reference the volume-scattering optimised physical model (Samuele et al., 2021) of the angular relationship between SAR imagery and terrain geometry to further modulate γ0: γ0f = γ0 / cosαr. Where, γ0f is the normalized backscattering coefficient for flat terrain. αr is the slope steepness angle in range direction, which is calculated based on terrain geometry modelling, with details provided in the Section S3 of the supplementary materials.

The Plot-Rice dataset contains 13,552 sample patches of 256 × 256 pixel size, with each patch including 10 feature time series and 1 plot-level label. The time series corresponds to the 12 months of 2023 through monthly averaging, establishing a uniform temporal baseline and eliminating anomalous fluctuations in the time sequences. For each month, 10 features are extracted from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2, including σ0VH, σ0VV, γ0VH, γ0VV, DpRVIc, RVI, NDVI, LSWI, true-colour images and false-colour images. During the slicing process, the patch overlap rate is set to 0.2 to maintain the completeness of rice plot boundaries. In addition, completely background (no rice distribution) patches or low-quality patches are excluded. All patches are shuffled and split into 60 % for training, 20 % for validation, and 20 % for testing model performance, thereby constructing the final Plot-Rice dataset for scientific deep learning.

entries.setup: 13,552 sample patches of 256 × 256 pixel size, each patch includes 10 feature time series and 1 plot-level label

evidence.original_text: The proposed dataset is evaluated using several state-of-the-art DL models, such as U-Net (Azad et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2022), Res U-Net (Diakogiannis et al., 2020; Onojeghuo et al., 2023), Deeplabv3+ (Chen et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2022), and HR-Net (Rasheed and Mahmood, 2023; Wang et al., 2020a). Over the past few years, these models have been widely applied to rice area extraction, yielding reliable results (Fu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024c; Wei et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2023). Therefore, these models are selected as benchmark models for training on the proposed dataset. In this study, the dice loss is used as the loss function to overcome the imbalance in training classes (Xu et al., 2023). Dice loss assigns equal importance to false positives and false negatives and is less sensitive to class imbalances. It is based on the dice similarity coefficient (DSC), which is defined as follows: DSC = 2|A ∩ B| / (|A| + |B|). where A represents the predicted segmentation (Rice), and B represents the actual label. | • | denotes the number of pixels. All models in the experiments are implemented using Torch 1.11, CUDA 11.7, and cuDNN 8.2, and are trained and evaluated on a Linux workstation equipped with an NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU with 24 GB of VRAM. Adam is selected as the optimizer, with an initial learning rate of 0.001 and a learning rate decay strategy. Each model has a batch size of 16, and training is conducted for 200 epochs.

entries.task: evaluation of datasets based on deep learning models
entries.setup: benchmark DL models (U-Net, Res U-Net, Deeplabv3+, HR-Net) trained on Plot-Rice dataset

evidence.original_text: The performance of the DL models is evaluated on the rice test sets across different continents using the optimal feature (DpRVIc), as shown in Table 4. Despite variations in the spatial morphology and temporal sequences of rice at each study site, different DL models consistently achieved accurate rice extraction. Oceania recorded the highest F1 score (84.05 %) across all models, attributed to the high mechanization of rice cultivation, which results in well-defined field boundaries that are easier to recognize. Additionally, the simplicity of the single-season cultivation model contributes to this outcome. Similarly, the Americas and Europe also exhibited high F1 scores. In Asia, the dense cultivation of rice led to a similarly high F1 score. Although Africa possesses a large area dedicated to rice cultivation, the relatively disorganized planting and management practices increased the difficulty of model classification.

entries.task: performance and transferability evaluation of DL models
entries.setup: DL models trained on Plot-Rice dataset tested on rice test sets across different continents

evidence.original_text: Fig. 11 presents the cross-spatiotemporal predictions of rice area in Louisiana, USA, for the year 2022, using the optimal feature and model (HR-Net) on data external to in the dataset. The predicted rice area is approximately 176 km2, which differs by only 8 km2 from the 2022 crop data layer (CDL, https://croplandcros.scinet.usda.gov/) statistical result of 184 km2. This difference is primarily attributed to the presence of more discrete pixels in CDL. These discrete pixels mainly originate from its decision tree-based classification method. Although CDL training data comes from ground truth data, the pixel-by-pixel classification nature of decision trees inevitably leads to some results appearing discrete, resulting in an overall accuracy of 80.9 % for 2022. In contrast, HR-Net trained on Plot-Rice uses plot-level samples as its learning foundation, utilizing convolutional neural networks to integrate spatial contextual information, which reduces the impact of discrete pixels and enhances the internal consistency of rice fields. Additionally, the resolution difference between CDL (30 m) and the predicted results (10 m) is also a source of error. The lower resolution of the CDL introduces additional bias.

entries.setup: HR-Net model trained on Plot-Rice dataset used to predict rice area in Louisiana, USA for 2022

The time series corresponds to the 12 months of 2023 through monthly averaging, establishing a uniform temporal baseline and eliminating anomalous fluctuations in the time sequences. For each month, 10 features are extracted from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2, including σ0 VH, σ0 VV, γ0 VH, γ0 VV, DpRVIc, RVI, NDVI, LSWI, true-colour images and false-colour images. During the slicing process, the patch overlap rate is set to 0.2 to maintain the completeness of rice plot boundaries. In addition, completely background (no rice distribution) patches or low-quality patches are excluded. All patches are shuffled and split into 60 % for training, 20 % for validation, and 20 % for testing model performance, thereby constructing the final Plot-Rice dataset for scientific deep learning.

entries.context: Plot-Rice benchmark dataset, patch size 256×256 pixels, covering 123 rice-growing regions across 20 countries globally

evidence.original_text: From Fig. 8, it is evident that Asian rice exhibits the highest fractal dimension, with a median of 1.58, and its overall distribution is more concentrated. This indicates that the spatial morphology of rice in Asia is the most complex, attributable to the region’s diverse planting patterns. Africa possesses the second-highest median fractal dimension at 1.39, suggesting a similarly high level of complexity in rice cultivation. In contrast, due to a higher degree of mechanization leading to more regular spatial forms, the Americas, Europe, and Oceania (mainly Australia) exhibit lower median fractal dimensions of 1.27, 1.11, and 1.20, respectively. The differences in fractal dimensions not only highlight the spatial morphological heterogeneity of rice on a global scale but also emphasize the necessity of considering this heterogeneity when extracting rice distribution information. Comprehending the spatial morphological differences of rice across regions enables researchers to more accurately extract rice distribution, thereby contributing to enhance refined agricultural applications.

entries.context: Spatial morphology heterogeneity analysis of rice fields across continents using box-counting method

evidence.original_text: To evaluate the robustness and usability of Plot-Rice, the performance of different models is assessed using various feature time series in the test set, as summarized in the Table 3. The results indicate that the temporal DpRVIc achieved the highest overall accuracy, with OA and F1 scores of 88.72 % and 82.30 %, respectively, significantly outperforming the RVI, which is also a vegetation index. Among the backscattering coefficients, both σ0 VH and γ0 VH exhibited better accuracy than σ0 VV and γ0 VV, suggesting that VH polarization is more favourable for rice classification than VV polarization. After RTC, there is a slight decrease in accuracy. This is because rice fields are predominantly distributed in flat terrain areas where local incidence angle variations are minimal, resulting in limited gains from RTC. Additionally, in some regions or during certain periods, the backscattering signals from rice may be attenuated by RTC, causing the temporal changes to be less significant. The NDVI and LSWI optical indices exhibited minor differences in accuracy, with LSWI overall outperforming NDVI. Despite applying cloud masking, true-colour imagery and the false-colour imagery yields relatively low accuracy, likely due to cloud interference. In summary, it is recommended to use the time series of VH polarization backscatter coefficient (σ0 VH or γ0 VH), DpRVIc, and LSWI for rice distribution extraction.

entries.compared_to: Different feature time series (DpRVIc, RVI, σ0 VH, γ0 VH, σ0 VV, γ0 VV, NDVI, LSWI, true-colour, false-colour)

Oceania recorded the highest F1 score (84.05 %) across all models, attributed to the high mechanization of rice cultivation, which results in well-defined field boundaries that are easier to recognize. Additionally, the simplicity of the single-season cultivation model contributes to this outcome. Similarly, the Americas and Europe also exhibited high F1 scores. In Asia, the dense cultivation of rice led to a similarly high F1 score. Although Africa possesses a large area dedicated to rice cultivation, the relatively disorganized planting and management practices increased the difficulty of model classification.

evidence.original_text: Fig. 11 presents the cross-spatiotemporal predictions of rice area in Louisiana, USA, for the year 2022, using the optimal feature and model (HR-Net) on data external to in the dataset. The predicted rice area is approximately 176 km2, which differs by only 8 km2 from the 2022 crop data layer (CDL, https://croplandcros.scinet.usda.gov/) statistical result of 184 km2. This difference is primarily attributed to the presence of more discrete pixels in CDL. These discrete pixels mainly originate from its decision tree-based classification method. Although CDL training data comes from ground truth data, the pixel-by-pixel classification nature of decision trees inevitably leads to some results appearing discrete, resulting in an overall accuracy of 80.9 % for 2022. In contrast, HR-Net trained on Plot-Rice uses plot-level samples as its learning foundation, utilizing convolutional neural networks to integrate spatial contextual information, which reduces the impact of discrete pixels and enhances the internal consistency of rice fields. Additionally, the resolution difference between CDL (30 m) and the predicted results (10 m) is also a source of error. The lower resolution of the CDL introduces additional bias.

Similar to the SPRI, the combination of VI, DI, and WI can yield a probability index between 0 and 1 indicating the likelihood of a pixel being rice, but in a more efficient and automated manner. A rice probability threshold of 0.9 is used to identify high-confidence rice pixels, which are subsequently utilized as training samples for the random forest model. The high-purity attributes of these samples ensured the accuracy and reliability of the model in pixel-level classification. In addition, the wetlands, which also contain aquatic vegetation, exhibit similar behavioural characteristics to rice in the backscatter coefficient time series. Relying solely on the three aforementioned indices is insufficient to effectively distinguish between wetlands and rice. Through time-series curve analysis, it can be observed that wetlands remain in a water environment for a longer period than rice, leading to a lower temporal mean backscatter coefficient. Therefore, the GWL_FCS30 wetland distribution map is used as a mask to further eliminate potential wetland pixels from the rice samples, while the temporal mean (σ0 mean) is introduced as a feature to distinguish between wetlands and rice. Finally, by combining the four extracted temporal features and the confirmed samples, a random forest model is applied to extract rice, resulting in pixel-level rice labels.

entries.context: Pixel-level rice label generation using temporal features (VI, DI, WI) and RF model

evidence.original_text: During the slicing process, the patch overlap rate is set to 0.2 to maintain the completeness of rice plot boundaries. In addition, completely background (no rice distribution) patches or low-quality patches are excluded. All patches are shuffled and split into 60 % for training, 20 % for validation, and 20 % for testing model performance, thereby constructing the final Plot-Rice dataset for scientific deep learning.

Satellite platforms have become the principal data source for large-scale rice mapping. Numerous studies investigated the advantages of integrating deep learning models with satellite data for rice mapping, but these studies are typically confined to specific regions and the quality of customized datasets varies. The absence of a global, standardized satellite dataset for rice mapping benchmarking has long resulted in both substantial redundant data processing efforts and challenges in evaluating new algorithms under a unified benchmark. To address these deficiencies, this paper introduces Plot-Rice v1.0, a global heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 images, along with an automated framework that integrates SAR temporal features and leverages the foundation image segmentation model SAM-2 to generate plot-level rice samples. This framework effectively addresses the limitation of SAM-2, which supports zero-shot segmentation but lacks classification capabilities. Plot-Rice comprehensively accounts for the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of rice, incorporating plot-level rice labels and corresponding multi-source feature time series from 20 countries globally. In terms of feature composition, Plot-Rice integrates multi-source features, including radar backscattering coefficients, normalized backscattering coefficients, radar vegetation indices, spectral indices, and spectral combinations, constructing time series for 12 months of 2023 to facilitate temporal analysis. Finally, using this dataset, a series of benchmark experiments is conducted, encompassing performance comparisons of models such as U-Net, Res U-Net, Deeplabv3+, and HR-Net under multi-source feature time series, and comparisons of rice distribution extraction performance across diverse countries. Several recommendations are summarized for artificial intelligence (AI) in rice mapping to serve as a reference, contributing to global food security solutions.

Currently, rice satellite datasets used for DL models encounter several challenges, including neglecting spatiotemporal heterogeneity, inconsistent quality, and significant access barriers. The long-standing scarcity of benchmark rice satellite datasets has led to repetitive data processing tasks and challenges in evaluating the performance of DL models under a unified standard. To address these gaps, this study introduces an automated plot-level rice sample generation framework leveraging SAM-2, which mitigates inefficiencies in manual labelling, enhances internal consistency, and resolves boundary ambiguities in existing datasets. By integrating time series analysis with machine learning, the framework overcomes SAM-2’s inherent classification constraints, enabling precise rice field classification. In addition, based on this framework, this study created Plot-Rice, the first reliable spatiotemporally heterogeneous rice benchmark dataset, to address current issues of limited heterogeneity, excessive customization, and insufficient resolution in rice datasets. This dataset seeks to establish benchmarks for DL models and enhances their global transferability. Derived from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 imagery, Plot-Rice provides time series of various commonly used features corresponding to 12 months of 2023, such as radar backscattering coefficients σ0, normalized radar backscattering coefficients γ0, RVI, DpRVIc, NDVI, LSWI, and spectral combination features to meet the needs of rice mapping and quantitative research. The proposed framework’s editable plot vectors, and the flexibility of feature composition further ensure scalability, allowing continuous updating of the Plot-Rice dataset and incorporation of new features. Experiments confirm Plot-Rice’s robustness in improving model transferability across spatiotemporal contexts. Additionally, based on the experimental results, it is recommended to use VH backscatter coefficients (σ0 VH or γ0 VH), DpRVIc, and LSWI as features for rice extraction, and to adopt a continuous multi-resolution fusion strategy in the design of DL models. Overall, the Plot-Rice’s reliability and flexibility position it as a scalable tool for global agricultural monitoring, with ongoing updates to address evolving food security challenges.

However, existing datasets used for DL in rice distribution extraction often suffer from limited heterogeneity, excessive customization, and inadequate resolution, constraining the development of rice mapping research and the evaluation of DL model performance. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop high-quality benchmark datasets for rice to address the requirements of rice distribution mapping research, particularly in the context of long time-series and large-scale applications. The limited heterogeneity of rice datasets constrains the transferability of DL models. From a global perspective, rice exhibits both spatial morphological heterogeneity and temporal variation heterogeneity. Influenced by topographical conditions, land planning, agricultural management practices, and other anthropogenic factors, the spatial morphology of rice differs across regions. In Asia, large plains or river valleys form extensive continuous rice fields. However, at a local scale, these fields are fragmented into irregular small plots. Spatially, rice fields display characteristics of local irregularity alongside an overall dense and extensive distribution. In developed regions such as Europe and America, agricultural planting is highly mechanized, thus rice fields there tend to be locally uniform and generally densely distributed. In less developed regions like Africa, agricultural planting ranges from small-scale family farms to large-scale commercial farms, leading to considerable internal spatial variability within rice fields, encompassing both large continuous areas and small, scattered plots, often of irregular shape. Additionally, climatic conditions, irrigation practices, and soil fertility are significant factors contributing to the temporal heterogeneity of rice cultivation systems. As latitude decreases, the intensity of rice cultivation increases, varying from single-season to triple-season rice. In regions like Southeast Asia, the coexistence of single-season, double-season, and triple-season rice is more common, further exacerbating the temporal heterogeneity of rice. Consequently, although DL can achieve high precision in extracting rice distribution over small areas, the lack of learning about the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of rice often results in a lack of transferability in DL models.

The excessive customization in datasets creates usage barriers that reduce both the usability of the dataset and the generalization capability of the DL models. The rice samples used for DL training mainly come from two sources: manual annotation and existing products. Many studies have acquired highly accurate rice samples for DL training through field surveys and manual annotations. However, due to the substantial costs of annotation, the scope of these studies is typically limited to farm-level or municipal-level areas, resulting in incomplete coverage of all planting conditions. This limitation leads to reduced generalization ability of DL models, making them difficult to transfer to other regions. Moreover, the datasets and classification models in such studies are excessively customized, posing significant barriers to reuse. The existence of multiple customized datasets within the same typical planting area has led to unnecessary duplication of effort and resource wastage. Some studies utilized existing rice distribution mapping products as labels and constructed datasets by extracting diverse features from satellite images for training DL models. However, the limited resolution of these existing products is insufficient to meet the needs for fine-scale rice mapping, failing to fully leverage the resolution advantages of the Sentinel satellite series, thus substantially hindering the development of large-scale rice distribution mapping. Additionally, existing large-scale rice mapping products generally employ classification methods such as dynamic time warping (DTW) and machine learning. These pixel-based classification methods neglect the spatial morphological characteristics of rice, resulting in indistinct boundaries of rice fields and causing a discretized distribution during the classification process. When such products are directly used for training DL models, they inevitably reduce the classification accuracy and the quality of prediction results. It is also important to consider the errors introduced during the registration and resampling processes of satellite data and product labels.
Overall, the Plot-Rice’s reliability and flexibility position it as a scalable tool for global agricultural monitoring, with ongoing updates to address evolving food security challenges.
